audit_trail.notes: All three reviewers participated in both cycles. Gemini upgraded the severity of the all-orders unitarity gap from minor to major in cycle 2 after reading the other reviews. Codex maintained a 'contested' verdict throughout while claude and gemini maintained 'promising'. The three major objections (all-orders assertion, missing BRST structure, unverified continuum fakeon extension) were independently raised by all three reviewers in cycle 2 and survived adversarial cross-review. The sheet-gluing objection is supported by claude and codex but disputed as overstated by gemini. Technical soundness scores converged downward in cycle 2 (from gemini's initial 8/10 to ~6/10 across reviewers). The paper bundle was truncated at 140000 of 141198 characters; the final ~1200 characters are absent but appear to be reference list tail, unlikely to affect substantive claims.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper attempts to show that a proposed theory of quantum gravity, built from fractional (non-integer power) versions of the wave operator, can be made mathematically consistent in the sense that probabilities are conserved across particle interactions. The review board found the tree-level spectral analysis — a detailed classification of which modes appear in the propagator and on which mathematical sheets — to be a genuine and useful technical contribution. However, the central claim of unitarity at *all* perturbative orders is extrapolated from one-loop examples without a rigorous inductive proof, and the extension to gauge and gravitational theories omits the BRST/ghost framework that such proofs require.
human_spotchecks: Theorem 2 coverage of gravity super-renormalizability range: does the rational-α condition (effectively requiring q ≤ p+1 for α = p/q irreducible) cover all α values needed for one-loop super-renormalizability of fractional gravity as specified in eqs. (13a)–(13d)? The HTML rendering of eqs. (122)–(124) is incomplete, preventing model verification.

Fakeon prescription for continuous mass spectra: is there an established theorem in the Anselmi–Piva fakeon literature (refs 32–37) that explicitly covers the case of a continuous family of complex-conjugate pairs with spectral mass parameter integrated from 0 to ∞, including commutativity of the average analytic continuation with the spectral integration?

Appendix B/C boundary cases: do Theorems 8, 14, and 20 for rational α — specifically the claimed exceptional values at odd p/q — correctly identify all cases where empty sheets fail to exist? The proofs involve modular arithmetic chains whose intermediate steps are obscured in the HTML rendering.

NH gauge/gravity positivity: does the integrand in eq. (109) remain positive after the derivative-vertex momentum factors (k²)^γ from eq. (103) are properly tracked through the unperformed mass integrations over μ₁ and μ₂?

BRST compatibility: can the scalar-toy-model unitarity argument in Sections 6.3–6.4 be lifted to the full gravitational action with gauge fixing, Faddeev–Popov ghosts, tensor projectors, and BRST identities, without violating Slavnov–Taylor relations when the fakeon prescription is imposed on the fractional propagator?
public_summary: Calcagni and Briscese study a class of quantum field theories — including a proposed theory of quantum gravity (fractional quantum gravity, FQG) — whose kinetic terms involve non-integer powers of the d'Alembertian operator. Unlike standard local QFT, these fractional kinetic terms render the propagator multi-valued, requiring analysis on a complex Riemann surface with multiple sheets. The paper's core technical achievement is a systematic classification of poles on this surface (Theorems 1–24 across Appendices B and C) for four variants of the fractional kinetic term (labeled HP, HS, HA, NH), together with a proof (Theorem 2) that there always exist Riemann sheets with no extra real or complex poles, covering the parameter ranges needed for super-renormalizability. On these sheets, the tree-level propagator decomposes into a standard IR mode plus a continuum of complex-conjugate pairs; the latter are argued to be purely virtual via the Anselmi–Piva fakeon prescription, preserving the physical spectrum as a single standard particle. One-loop calculations for scalar and schematic gauge/gravity models are provided to illustrate the mechanism. The paper additionally argues that different definitions of the fractional kinetic term belong to the same universality class, sharing the same physical asymptotic spectrum. These results, if fully substantiated, would substantially simplify and firm up the theoretical foundations of FQG compared to earlier splitting-regularization approaches.
reviewer_disagreements: Severity of the all-orders unitarity gap: gemini initially rated this 'minor' with 'would_change_verdict_if_true: false' in cycle 1, while claude and codex rated it 'major' throughout. Gemini upgraded to 'major' in cycle 2 after cross-review, converging with the other reviewers.

Overall verdict: codex recommended 'contested' across both cycles, arguing the headline all-orders and gravity claims outrun the presented proofs. Claude and gemini recommended 'promising' throughout, emphasizing that the Riemann-sheet classification and spectral decomposition are genuine publishable contributions independent of whether the all-orders claim is fully established.

Sheet-gluing in Theorem 2: gemini characterized selecting branches across a discontinuity as 'standard procedure in multi-valued complex functions' and considered this objection overstated; claude and codex flagged it as requiring explicit justification that the composite sheet is a globally admissible propagator branch.
Technical soundness scoring diverged sharply in cycle 1: gemini scored 8/10, claude scored 6/10, and codex scored 5.4/10. All three converged toward the 5.5–6 range in cycle 2 after cross-review.

strongest_objections.objection: The 'all-orders' perturbative unitarity claim is not derived — it is repeatedly asserted as 'straightforward' or 'immediate' following one-loop scalar examples, without supplying an inductive diagrammatic argument for arbitrary multi-loop topologies, overlapping divergences, or multiple continuum fakeon integrations in nonlocal fractional theories.

The gauge and gravity unitarity arguments (Sections 6.3–6.4) model the graviton and gauge fields as scalar toy models with trivial tensorial structure, entirely omitting the BRST/Slavnov–Taylor identities, Faddeev–Popov ghost contributions, gauge-fixing, and tensor projectors required to establish perturbative unitarity in gauge theories and gravity. Physical-polarization cancellations are not verified.

The Anselmi–Piva fakeon prescription is cited for theories with a finite number of isolated complex poles at fixed masses; the paper applies it to a continuum of complex-conjugate pairs with mass parameter integrated from 0 to ∞ (eq. 58). The commutativity of mass integration, loop integration, and average analytic continuation is asserted without stating convergence hypotheses or excluding endpoint or pinch contributions that could pollute the optical theorem.

In the NH gauge/gravity one-loop calculation (Section 6.4), the mass integrals in eq. (105) are explicitly left unperformed ('we do not perform the rest of the calculation here'), yet a unitarity conclusion is drawn. The positivity of the spectral density under eq. (109) is asserted without tracking the derivative-vertex momentum factors (k²)^γ through the continuum mass integration.

The proof of Theorem 2 relies on 'arbitrarily gluing' two half-planes (Σ(j₊) and Σ(j₋)) into a Riemann sheet without explicitly verifying that every such composite sheet is a globally admissible branch of the original multi-valued propagator compatible with the branch-cut prescription and physical analytic continuation.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Appendix B, Theorem 2 proof; analogous Theorems 8, 14, 20

strongest_supported_claims: The tree-level propagator spectral decomposition (eq. 84) isolates a single standard massless/massive mode as the only on-shell asymptotic state, with all complex-conjugate pairs and continuum modes off-shell; this follows directly from the contour calculations in Sections 3–4 and is independently accepted by all three reviewers.

For the HP kinetic-term definition, Theorem 2 establishes the existence of Riemann sheets empty of all real and complex poles for the fractional exponent in the super-renormalizable range; the irrational-α case is handled by Weyl equidistribution and the rational case by explicit arithmetic conditions.

Different fractional kinetic-term definitions (HP, HS, HA, NH) yield the same physical asymptotic spectrum for α > 0, differing only in the distribution of purely virtual modes; the comparative analysis in Section 5 is consistent across all reviewers.

Super-renormalizability for specific ranges of the fractional exponent α is accepted as established prior work (Calcagni:2022shb), with the BPHZ power-counting argument unchanged by the hermitian vs. non-hermitian distinction.

The non-hermitian (NH) scalar model maintains perturbative unitarity without the fakeon prescription when the spectral density positivity condition (eq. 80) holds; the explicit one-loop scalar calculation in Section 6.2 is agreed to be correct by all reviewers.
technical_takeaway: The paper performs a systematic Riemann-surface analysis of the tree-level propagator for self-adjoint fractional d'Alembertians, classifying poles across four kinetic-term variants (HP, HS, HA, NH) and proving that pole-free Riemann sheets exist in the super-renormalizable parameter range (Theorem 2). The physical spectrum is argued to consist of a single standard massless/massive mode, with all complex-conjugate pairs and continuum modes relegated to a purely virtual sector via the Anselmi–Piva fakeon prescription. Unitarity at all perturbative orders is claimed but demonstrated explicitly only at one loop for scalar toy models and schematic derivative-vertex models; the gauge/gravity extension suppresses BRST/ghost structure and the continuum-fakeon step requires commuting mass integration with average analytic continuation under conditions that are stated but not verified.
